coredata.dc:title: Biomass-based magnetic fluorescent nanoparticles: One-step scalable synthesis, application as drug carriers and mechanism study
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lei
Wang, Feijun
Shao, Ziqiang
coredata.dc:description: A biomass-based magnetic fluorescent nanoparticle (MFNPs) was successively in situ synthesized via a one-step high-gravity approach, which constructed by a magnetic core of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the fluorescent marker of carbon dots (CDs), and shells of chitosan (CS). The obtained MFNPs had a 10 nm average diameter and narrow particle size distribution, low cytotoxicity, superior fluorescent emission and superparamagnetic properties. The encapsulating and release 5-fluorouracil experiments confirmed that the introduction of CS/CDs effectively improved the drug loading capacity. Mechanism and kinetic studies proved that: (i) the monolayer adsorption was the main sorption mode under the studied conditions; (ii) the whole adsorption process was controlled by intra-liquid diffusion mass transfer and governed by chemisorption; and (iii) the release process was controlled by Fickian diffusion. These results demonstrated this method to one-step continuously produce MFNPs and the construction of non-toxic nanostructure possessed great superiority in currently Nano-delivery systems, which would show high application value in targeted drug delivery, magnetic fluid hyperthermia treatment, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), in vitro testing and relative research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3O4 nanoparticles
Chitosan
High-gravity reactive precipitation method
5-Fluorouracil
Kinetics

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Fe3O4@CS/CDs NPs in IS-RPB
Effect of different factors on 5-FU adsorption capacity
Modeling of equilibrium data and thermodynamic parameters
Drug release and coexisting substances influence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation process of MFNPs.

TEM image of Fe3O4 (a) and Fe3O4@CS/CDs (FCCs) (b), inset of (a) is corresponding diameter distributions, insets of (b) are local HRTEM image, selected area electron diffraction pattern and corresponding diameter distributions. HRTEM image of FCCs (c). (d) Elemental distribution of C (red), Fe(yellow), N (blue) and O (green) of FCCs. XRD patterns (e) and FT-IR spectra (f) of the samples. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

((a) Magnetization curve of samples,inset is the DLS size distribution of samples. (b) Maximum emission of CDs and FCCs wavelength with different excitation wavelength, inset is the digital photos of CDs dispersion under different excitation wavelength. (c) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore-size distribution of samples. (d) MTT assay of HeLa and HeLung cell viability after incubation with the FCCs at different concentrations for 48 h at 37 °C.

Laser scanning confocal images of representative HeLung (top) and HeLa cells (bottom) incubated with blank (a, e) and MFNPs with a concentration of 500 μg/mL for 6 h (b, f), 12 h (c, g) and 24 h (d, h), respectively. Excitation laser wavelength = 405 nm.
Effect of pH values on adsorption capacity (a). Effect of pH value on zeta potentials of the samples (b). Effect of adsorbent dosage (c) and sample volume (d) on the adsorption of 5-FU.

Equilibrium data for 5-FU sorption onto Fe3O4 (a) and FCCs (d), and the linear fitting results of Langmuir (b, e) and Freundlich (c, f) models. Inset of (a) and (d) is the linear fitting results of Van’t Hoff equation when Fe3O4 and FCCs are the carriers, respectively.

(a) Effect of contact time on equilibrium adsorption capacity of NPs. (b) Linear fitting results of pseudo-first-order kinetic model. (c) Linear fitting results of pseudo-second-order kinetic model. (d) Linear fitting results of intraparticle diffusion model.

(a) Release curve of 5-FU-loaded NPs in different PBS (pH 5.5 and 7.4). (b) Higuchi model (M t/M ∞ versus t1/2) linear fitting results at different pH values at 37 °C. (c) Korsmeyer-Peppas model (ln(M t/M ∞) versus lnt) linear fitting results at different pH values at 37 °C. (d) Effect of different coexisting ions on 5-FU release (release time is 30 min).
Kinetic parameters of 5-FU sorption onto the NPs.
Loading kinetic parameters and thermodynamic parameters of 5-FU sorption onto the NPs.

head.ce:title: Biomass-based magnetic fluorescent nanoparticles: One-step scalable synthesis, application as drug carriers and mechanism study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Engineering Research Centre of Cellulose and Its Derivatives, Beijing 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Engineering Research Centre of Cellulose and Its Derivatives

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A biomass-based magnetic fluorescent nanoparticle (MFNPs) was successively in situ synthesized via a one-step high-gravity approach, which constructed by a magnetic core of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the fluorescent marker of carbon dots (CDs), and shells of chitosan (CS). The obtained MFNPs had a 10 nm average diameter and narrow particle size distribution, low cytotoxicity, superior fluorescent emission and superparamagnetic properties. The encapsulating and release 5-fluorouracil experiments confirmed that the introduction of CS/CDs effectively improved the drug loading capacity. Mechanism and kinetic studies proved that: (i) the monolayer adsorption was the main sorption mode under the studied conditions; (ii) the whole adsorption process was controlled by intra-liquid diffusion mass transfer and governed by chemisorption; and (iii) the release process was controlled by Fickian diffusion. These results demonstrated this method to one-step continuously produce MFNPs and the construction of non-toxic nanostructure possessed great superiority in currently Nano-delivery systems, which would show high application value in targeted drug delivery, magnetic fluid hyperthermia treatment, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), in vitro testing and relative research.

ce:section.ce:para: To address the problem of drug toxicity and targeted transport in clinical treatment, currently, important research progress has been made in Nano-devices design for targeting and transport functions (El-Hag Ali & AlArifi, 2009; Singh, Sharma, & Dhiman, 2017; Zamora-Mora et al., 2017). A large variety of Nano-delivery systems (NDS) with different morphologies and structures based on nanobiologic materials have been successfully developed and applied as drug carriers (Alibolandi, Mohammadi, Taghdisi, Ramezani, & Abnous, 2017; Kono, Otaka, & Ozaki, 2014; Seeli & Prabaharan, 2017; Yang et al., 2017). Among these systems, core-shell nanoparticles (NPs) are considered as one of the most promising candidates for targeted drug delivery and controlled release (Lee et al., 2017; Tan et al., 2017). The significant benefits of core-shell NPs systems are small size, high loading capacity and adjustability of functionality (Zhang et al., 2015). By incorporation of various charged species can be provided with multi-functionality and responsibility to various external stimuli (Li, Li, & Chen, 2015; Lu et al., 2017). In recent years, attribute to fast magnetic response and the photoluminescence (PL) ability, magnetic fluorescent nanoparticles (MFNPs) have been widely used in the field of cancer detection and treatment, such as multimodality imaging (Lu et al., 2015), drug detection (Ming et al., 2017), drug delivery (Qi et al., 2011) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) imaging (Wang et al., 2014). As a novel type of NDS, they can directly guide the drug to the lesion by the magnetic drive, and real-time monitor the drug location and concentration by fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (Zhang & Khamesee, 2017).

The MFNPs conventionally have three modules: a permanent magnetic center, the polyelectrolyte shells and the fluorescent marking materials. Permanent magnetic materials such as Nano iron oxide have attracted intensive attentions because of their excellent physical and chemical properties (Fan, Li, Zhou, & Liu, 2016; Fan, Zhou, Gao, & Liu, 2016), including high magnetic saturation strength, strong adsorption ability, good biosafety, excellent surface activity and regular exclusion of in vitro. Unfortunately, the aggregates of magnetic NPs are inevitable during synthesis, separation and modification process. These processes often induce a large increase in size and significant surface energy fade, and thereby limiting their commercial applications (Fan, Zhou, Qi, & Liu, 2016). Especially in NDS, the aggregates may lead to fluorescent quenching, drug carrying capacity attenuating or even disappearing (Shen, Tang, Zhang, Chen, & Zhang, 2012). On the other hand, most fluorescent marking material like rare earth luminescent materials and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) (Li, Wang, Shi, Zhang, & Shao, 2015), part of polyelectrolyte shells like NH2-PEG-COOH and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) (Ehi-Eromosele, Ita, & Iweala, 2017), have certain biological toxicity and environmental load. Thus, although many reports suggested the excellent drug delivery properties of MFNPs, especially for the anticancer drugs, the development of intelligent economical carriers is still a huge dilemma. Novel alternative eco-friendly polyelectrolytes and non-toxic fluorescent materials like carbon dots (CDs) and biomass like chitosan (CS) are expected to improve these issues (Li et al., 2017; Rasoulzadeh & Namazi, 2017; Sathuvan et al., 2017). CS, as a typical carbohydrate polymer, has excellent properties such as biodegradability and low toxicity (Kong et al., 2017; Racine, Costa, Bayma-Pecit, Texier, & Auzely-Velty, 2017). CDs are provided with excellent optical properties, small size, good biocompatibility, easy-to-function surface, superior stability, and multiple wavelength fluorescence response capability (Assaad, Wang, Zhu, & Mateescu, 2011; Yao et al., 2017). These superior properties make them extremely attracting in diverse biomedical applications (Mansur et al., 2017). Nevertheless, how to design and fabricate the systems with reduced cost, wide-adapted drug encapsulation capacity and distinguished therapeutic efficacy are still long-standing challenges (Ma, Sun, & Chen, 2017). The high-gravity technology (HGT), which has realized the combination of process intensification (PI) and co-precipitation method (Jiao et al., 2017), is expected to achieve continuous scalable preparation of MFNPs with narrow size dispersion and nanometer scale (Fan, Zhou and Qi et al., 2016; Leng et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2017). The Impinging Stream-Rotating Packed Bed (IS-RPB), as a typical HGT equipment, can strongly intensify micro-mixing and mass-transfer, which is very suitable to achieve uniform super-saturation and homogeneous nucleation (Fan and Li et al., 2016). Previous work also has successfully achieved the production of Nano Fe3O4 in IS-RPB and applied for electrochemical detection (Fan, Zhou, Jiao, Qi, & Liu, 2017).

In this paper, a new type of MFNPs with superparamagnetic Fe3O4 primary nanocrystals as nuclei and CS carrying CDs as shells were constructed based on HGT approaches. This method is expected to achieve one-step scalable preparation of MFNPs and apply them for drug delivery. 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU), as a pyrimidine analogue, was chosen to be the target drug because of it has been one of the major clinically applied anticancer agents for treating various tumors (Ehi-Eromosele et al., 2017). Based on the theoretical understanding and interpretation of the comparative phenomenon for the design and synthesis of bifunctional NPs along with concurrent endeavors to promote drug transport kinetics and avoid toxic adverse effects have been reported to a lesser extent (Esmaeili & Haseli, 2017; Gayathri et al., 2017), the equilibrium, loading kinetics and thermodynamic experiments were investigated in a batch system, and two classical models of Higuchi model and Korsmeyer-Peppas mode were applied to investigate the release kinetics.

The ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetic acid (CH3COOH), citric acid, urea, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) were purchased from National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Chitosan (CS, deacetylation higher than 90%) with the average molecular weight of 161 kDa was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dialysis tubing with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1 and 100 kDa and a flat diameter of 28 mm was purchased from Biotopped (Viskase, US). 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) was bought from Sigma-Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Fe3O4@CS/CDs NPs in IS-RPB
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared using the microwave method via the reaction between urea and citric acid (Simões, Leitão, & da Silva, 2016). 3 g urea and 3 g citric acid were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. The solution was heated by a microwave oven for 45 min (750W). In this step, the solution turned from a colorless liquid to a brown or blackish brown cluster. Then, the obtained solid was transferred to the vacuum oven and be heated at 60 °C for 1 h to remove the residual small molecules. After centrifuge separation (3000 r/min, 20 min) and dialysis purification (MWCO = 1 kDa) for 48 h, the solution of carbon dots was finally obtained. The Fe3O4@CS/CDs NPs were in situ synthesized via one-step high-gravity reactive precipitation of FeCl2·4H2O and FeCl3·6H2O by NaOH solution in the presence of CS and CDs in IS-RPB. A typical preparation procedure was followed: FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O and CS, with a mass ratio of 2.42:1:0.57, were dissolved in 0.5 L of deionized water, the pH value was adjusted to 5.0 before adding CS powder, which was placed in stirred tank 1. The NaOH concentration was dependent on the molar ratio of OH− to Fe2+ of 6.0, which was dissolved in 0.5 L of 0.5 mg L−1 CDs solution and placed in stirred tank 2. Then the solutions in tank 1 and tank 2 were continuously and simultaneously pumped into different liquid distributors in the high-gravity equipment at the same liquid flow rate of 60 L h−1 at 80 °C (Fig. 1 ). The equipment was continuously bubbled with nitrogen to ensure that there was no dissolved oxygen in the system. The operating conditions used in this study were a high-gravity factor of 65.32, and a FeCl3·6H2O concentration of 0.321 mol L−1. Finally, Fe3O4@CS/CDs NPs (FCCs) were magnetically separated, washed several times using ultrapure water and ethanol until neutral pH value, and freeze-dried. As a comparison, a reaction process without adding CDs and CS was established simultaneously.

MTT assay was used to characterize in vitro cytotoxicity of the MFNPs using human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) and human embryo lung (HeLung) normal cells. In short, the cells were incubated with the MFNPs at different concentrations from 10 to 200 μg mL−1 at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well (with 200 μL culture medium). After maintained for 48 h (5% CO2, 37 °C), 20 μL of MTT stock solution was added to each well, and the cells were further incubated for 4 h. Finally, 0.15 mL of DMSO was used to completely replaced the medium in each well. The absorbance at 490 nm was analyzed via Cell Counting Kit-8 to obtain the viability of the cells.

The HeLung (normal) cells and HeLa (cancer) cell in vitro fluorescence images were detected on laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM, Olympus, FV-300, IX71) under an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. Cells were seeded on a 6-well plate at 37 °C at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well for 24 h. Then, the MFNPs (500 μg/mL) were added to the wells. After incubation, these MFNPs-loaded cells were washed three times with 10 mM PBS to remove the free nanoparticles for further fluorescence imaging.

The loading experiments were carried out in flasks containing the adsorbent in 5-FU solutions on a rotary shaker at 200 rpm for 2.5 h. The 5-FU sorption onto the Fe3O4 and FCCs was carried out as functions of initial concentration (10–800 mg L−1), pH value (3–8), sample volume (250–500 mL), adsorbent dosage (0.5–2.5 g L−1), temperature (25–45 °C) and contact time (0–150 min) in a batch system. Each experiment was repeated triplicate and the results were given as averages. The loading capacity (q e (mg g−1)) of 5-FU is calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
q e
=
(
c 0
−
c e
)
V
1000 M
where c 0 and c e are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of 5-FU solution in mg L−1, V is the volume of the solution in mL and M is the weight of the adsorbent in g. The 5-FU-loaded NPs were dispersed in 5 mL buffer solution (pH 5.5 and 7.4) in dialysis bags (MWCO100 kDa) and immersed in 50 mL phosphate buffered saline (PBS) at 37 °C with a constant shaking rate of 200 rpm. The cumulative release (%) of 5-FU from the NPs was expressed as the total percentage of 5-FU released at time t versus the mass of 5-FU encapsulated in the NPs (Wang et al., 2017). The drug loading and release mediums (1 mL) were withdrawn for UV–vis absorption analysis (Lambda35, PerkinElmer) at a wavelength of 265 nm.

The morphology of NPs was determined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, TECNAI G2 F20) together with the energy-dispersive spectrometry mapping. The average diameter and diameter distribution of nanoparticles were obtained with an image analyzer (Image-Proplus, Media Cybernetics). From each image, at least 100 different NPs segments were randomly selected and their diameters were measured to generate an average diameter. The crystalline structure of different samples was verified using wide-angle XRD on a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer equipped with a Cu Kα source operating at 40 kV and 40 mA at a scan rate of 0.02° s−1. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of samples were recorded using a PerkinElmer Spectrum Two equipment with a 2 cm−1 resolution in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 for 4 scans. PL spectra data were determined via a steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000) at room temperature; the excitation wavelength was selected at 320–500 nm. The magnetic properties of NPs were carried out using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, 7407 Lakeshore Instrument Plant). The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore-size distribution (Quantachrome NOVA-4000e) was used to characterize the pore structure. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, UK) was applied to determine the particle size distribution of nanoparticles. The zeta potentials were determined using a zeta potential analyzer (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments Corporation), the test was repeated triplicate and the results were given as averages.

The morphology of NPs obtained from the IS-RPB were investigated using TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 2 a–c). The Fe3O4 and FCCs NPs exhibited relatively small average particle sizes of 6 nm and 10 nm with narrow size distributions, respectively which is much lower than the reported NDS (Li, Guo, Meng, & Zhang, 2011). This can be explained by the PI effect provided by the high-gravity environment. The IS-RPB equipment can generate strongly micro-mixing, uniform supersaturating and homogeneous nucleation (Fan and Li et al., 2016), which can facilitate magnetic particle size reduction and distribution narrowing down. Hence, CS and CDs can be adsorbed to the surface of nanosized magnetic core and assembled into a core-shell structure (Fan et al., 2017). The local HRTEM images of FCCs exhibited the lattice fringes of individual FCCs with a d spacing of 0.35 nm could be assigned to the (101) lattice planes of Fe3O4, which was evidenced by the XRD analyses discussed later. The elemental mapping results of FCCs (Fig. 2d) recorded the distribution of C, Fe, N, and O elements, which can indicate the CS has incorporated on the Fe3O4.

For further investigation into crystallinity of the MFNPs, XRD analysis was carried out, and the XRD patterns of CDs, CS, Fe3O4 and FCCs were exhibited in Fig. 2e. The diffraction peaks center in CDs at 2 θ = 28.0° can be referred to amorphous carbon structure (Simões et al., 2016). Chitosan has a significant diffraction peak at 2 θ = 20.6°, which indicates that only Form II crystals are present in the chitosan powder (L. Wu, Wang, Luo, & Dai, 2009). The diffraction peaks appear in FCCs at 2 θ = 30.11°, 35.41°, 43.11°, 53.41°, 57.01°, and 62.61° can correspond to the crystal planes of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) of magnetite Fe3O4 (JCPDS11-0614), respectively (Fan, Zhou and Qi et al., 2016). The patterns of Fe3O4 and FCCs are almost identified, however, a 2.0° increase appear in that of FCCs, which may be interpreted as that a few O atoms are slightly incorporated into the Fe3O4 crystal lattice (Wu et al., 2009).

The FTIR spectra of the as-prepared CDs, CS, Fe3O4 and FCCs were shown in Fig. 2f. As seen in the spectrum of CDs, two significant absorption peaks at 1690 cm−1 and 1360 cm−1 corresponding to the asymmetric vibrational peak and symmetrical vibrational peak of COOH can be observed, which illustrates that the surface of CDs contains hydroxyl groups because of the incomplete pyrolysis of citric acid (Simões et al., 2016). In addition, the absorption band of 3100–3500 cm−1 can be assigned to vibration absorption band of OH; the absorption band of 1350–1460 cm−1 can be attributed to vibration absorption band of CH2; the absorption band of 1600–1770 cm−1 can correspond to the absorption band of CO. In the FCCs spectrum, the absorption peak at 2926 cm−1 can be assigned to CH alkane stretching vibration, the absorption peak at 2850 cm−1 can correspond to CH3 stretching vibration of chitosan residues, the peak at 1556 cm−1 can be attributed to NH2 deformation vibration. Notably, the characteristic absorption peaks of CH3 and NH2 in the FCCs spectrum shifts to long wavelengths compared to that of the pristine CS, which can be explained by the chemical bond between CS and Fe3O4 NPs. Thus, combine with the results of EDS and XRD, we believe that the CS and CDs have successfully modified on the Fe3O4 surface.

Fig. 3 a presented the magnetic properties of the Fe3O4 and FCCs. Both samples have almost reversible hysteresis behavior and zero coercivity, which demonstrates that the MFNPs maintain a typical of superparamagnetic behavior. Moreover, the saturation magnetization of the as-prepared Fe3O4 and FCCs are 71.23 emu g−1 and 58.1 emu g−1, respectively, which illuminates that saturation magnetization decline after non-magnetic CS/CDs incorporation. It was reported that NPs possessing saturation magnetizations above 16.3 emu g−1 could be separated from suspensions using a magnet (Fan, Zhou and Qi et al., 2016), thus the MFNPs can be easily separated from aqueous suspensions and be magnetically drivable (Zhang & Khamesee, 2017). Inset of Fig. 3a showed the DLS size distribution of CDs, Fe3O4, and FCCs samples. Their corresponding average particle sizes are 16.6, 25.4, and 26.3 nm, respectively, which are smaller than TEM results. These results could be explained as the determination of DLS is the hydraulic radius of NPs. The variation of the maximum emission wavelength with excitation wavelength were determined and shown in Fig. 3b. As seen, the CDs aqueous solution exhibits relatively high fluorescence emission intensity and typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristics. When the excitation wavelength changes from 320 nm to 480 nm, the emission peak also migrated from 440 nm (blue light) to 570 nm (yellow light), which can be attributed to band-gap transitions in conjugated π-domains, surface groups, defects and surface states (Yang et al., 2009). The FCCs prepared using the HGT method maintain the wavelength-dependent properties (inset of Fig. 3b), however, the fluorescence intensity is much lower than that of CDs. The possible explanations are: (i) only a few CDs is wrapped in FCCs and (ii) the luminescence property of CDs is quenched in presence of Fe3+. In consideration of previous reports, the fluorescence emission intensity was enough to be detected in vivo (Fu et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2013; Voronin et al., 2017; Yen et al., 2013), thus, this MFNPs prepared via HGT method can provide a distinct fluorescence intensity for fluorescence imaging.

Fig. 3c showed the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution of Fe3O4 and FCCs. Both two curves can be classified as type-IV isotherm loops (Li et al., 2016). The BET specific surface area of the FCCs samples are 59.58 m−2 g−1, and the BJH desorption cumulative pore volume and the average pore size are 0.13 cm−3 g−1 and 77.52 nm, respectively. The BET specific surface area, BJH desorption cumulative pore volume and the average pore size increase after the introduction of CS/CDs, which indicates that the polymer-layer and CDs can effectively ameliorate the surface texture properties of MFNPs. These porous structures of the as-prepared MFNPs would provide ideal physical space and nanoscale channels for drug loading and release, therefore, they are potentially suitable for drug delivery (Gao et al., 2017). The in vitro cytotoxicity of FCCs was investigated using conducting MTT assays against HeLa and HeLung cells. As shown in Fig. 3d, incubation with various concentrations of FCCs (10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 and 1000 μg mL−1) for 48 h have no obvious negative impacts on the viability of the two cell types. Thus, the FCCs sample is substantially free of cytotoxicity as all raw materials have high biocompatibility (Yao et al., 2017), which facilitates potential biological applications. CLSM images allow to obtain further information on cytotoxicity and cellular uptake of the MFNPs (Shen et al., 2012; Zamora-Mora et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 4 , both cells exhibit morphological integrity after a long-period incubation, which further indicates the biocompatibility of the MFNPs (Yao et al., 2017). The fluorescence signals show punctuated cytoplasmic distribution, which demonstrates the NPs can enter the endolysosomal system and be mainly located in the cytoplasm (Tan et al., 2017). An ideal nanocarrier should promote the sorption and permeation of cargos to tumor tissues to achieve the tumor-targeted therapy, to affirm whether the MFNPs could efficiently assist 5-FU to accumulate in tumor, the biodistribution of the MFNPs in HeLung and HeLa cells was continually recorded. As shown in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity is clearly determined and comparable higher than HeLung cells in the Hela tumor cells after incubation, which demonstrates the main way of nanocarriers enter the cells was through the EPR effect (Wei et al., 2013; Yao et al., 2017). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity exhibits no signs of abating even for 24 h, which suggests that MFNPs would play an important role in prolonging the 5-FU retention time in the intestinal tract.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different factors on 5-FU adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the 5-FU loading capacity using Fe3O4 and FCCs was investigated in the pH range of 3 ∼ 8 for the initial concentration of 100 mg L−1, time of 150 min, a sample volume of 250 mL, the adsorbent dosage of 0.5 g L−1 and 25 °C (Fig. 5 a). As shown, the loading capacity of 5-FU increased by increasing pH values first and then decreased slightly. The maximum adsorption capacity of Fe3O4 and FCCs occurred approximately at pH 5.0 and 7.0. At low pH values, the amine groups on the MFNP surfaces tended to protonated, which led to an electrostatic repulsion of 5-FU. Meanwhile, the competition between the H+ ions and 5-FU for active sites was fierce in this solution. Nevertheless, at higher pH values, the concentration of H+ ions in the solution declined and MFNP surface was deprotonated, which led to the 5-FU loading increase (Beheshti, Irani, Hosseini, Rahimi, & Aliabadi, 2016). When the pH values were higher than 7.0, a significant agglomerate existed between the NPs. Fig. 5b showed the variation of the zeta potential of FCCs nanocomposites, Fe3O4 NPs and 5-FU molecule in the pH range 3–10. The isoelectric points appearred at pH 5.8 and 7.1 for pristine Fe3O4 and FCCs nanocomposites. The zeta potential was 42.0 mV at pH 3 and decline to −36.0 mV at pH 10 for Fe3O4 NPs. Attribute to the presence of CS/CDs in the nanocomposites, the zeta potential was found to be 19.2 mV at pH 3 and decreases to −31 mV at pH 10. The negative zeta potential value of 5-FU molecule was observed at pH 3–10. The maximum zeta potential difference of Fe3O4 NPs and 5-FU existed at pH 5.0, while the maximum zeta potential difference of FCCs nanocomposites and 5-FU appeared at pH 7. Therefore, efficient adsorption of 5-FU was found at pH 5.0 onto FCCs nanocomposites and at pH 7.0 onto Fe3O4 NPs due to electrostatic attraction between the anionic 5-FU molecules and positive surface charge of the adsorbents (Fan et al., 2017).

The effect of adsorbent dosage on the amount of adsorption under the initial concentration of 100 mg L−1, pH value of 7.0, a sample volume of 250 mL, the temperature of 25 °C, and contact time of 150 min was shown in Fig. 5c. As seen, the 5-FU adsorption amount gradually decreased with the increase of the adsorbent dosage, which indicated that the adsorption density of the 5-FU on the surface of adsorbents was high under low adsorbent dosage. With the increase of the adsorbent dosage, the excessive adsorbent would lead to the agglomeration of NPs. Meanwhile, the relatively low concentration of 5-FU in the liquid phase was not favorable for the intra-liquid mass transfer. Thus, the adsorption amount of 5-FU was decreased with the adsorbent dosage, which was also reported in the analogous literature (Beyers et al., 2009).

The varies of adsorption amount with sample volume was demonstrated in Fig. 5d. The experiment was carried out as functions of the initial concentration of 100 mg L−1, a pH value of 7.0, the adsorbent dosage of 0.5 g L−1, the temperature of 25 °C, and contact time of 150 min. With the increase of sample volume, the adsorption capacity of 5-FU on adsorbents decreased slightly. The possible reason was that the mixing strength of adsorbent and 5-FU would decrease on a rotary shaker with constant shaking frequency, which resulted in the decrease of mass transfer efficiency at the solid-liquid interface. When the size of the sample volume increased from 250 mL to 500 mL, the adsorption amount of FCCs only decreased by 2%, which indicated that the negative amplification effect was not significant in this system. Thus, the prepared MFNPs have great potential in the treatment of large-scale wastewater.

The effect of contact time on the sorption of 5-FU onto the NPs in the initial concentration of 100 mg L−1, pH of 5 and 25 °C was depicted in Fig. 6 a. As shown, the process of drug sorption onto the NPs achieved the equilibrium state after approximately 120 min. More than 80% of total sorption capacity of 5-FU reached within the first 60 min. Kinetic models namely pseudo-first-order (Lagergren, 1898), pseudo-second-order kinetic (Ho & McKay, 1999) and intraparticle diffusion models (Beheshti et al., 2016) (Eqs. (2–4)) were employed to describe the kinetic data:
(2)
q t
=
q e
( 1-exp
( −
k 1
) )
(3)
q t
=
k 2
q e 2
t
1 +
k 2
q e
t
(4) qt = kit1/2 + C
where q t and q e (mg g−1) are the adsorption capacity at time t and equilibrium time, respectively; k 1 (min−1) is the pseudo-first order model rate constant; k 2 (g mg−1 min−1) is the pseudo-second-order adsorption rate constant; k i ((mg g−1 min−1) is the intra-particle diffusion adsorption rate constant; C is the intra-particle diffusion adsorption constant. The parameters obtained by linear fitting (Fig. 6b and c) are presented in Table 1 . As shown, both equations of pseudo-first-order (R 2 > 0.95) and pseudo-second-order (R 2 > 0.99) were suitable to describe the experimental data, indicating that the overall adsorption process was controlled by diffusion mass transfer, as well as governed by chemisorption (sharing or exchange of electrons between the adsorbent and adsorbate) (Beheshti et al., 2016). In this adsorption, adsorption rate is usually dependent on the number of available adsorption sites on the surface of the adsorbent and eventually controlled by the attachment of the adsorbates on the surface. Furthermore, the higher pseudo-second-order regression correlation coefficients (R 2 > 0.999) using FCCs as absorbent illustrated the chemisorption was more distinct than Fe3O4. This can be explained as the CS and CDs can provide more surface active sites. Generally, the adsorption process is controlled by the intra-liquid mass transfer rate and the intra-particles mass transfer rate, the fitting degree intra-particle diffusion models can directly show which factor dominates the adsorption rate. As shown in Fig. 6d and Table 1, the relatively low correlation coefficients (R 2 > 0.96) demonstrated that the intra-particle diffusion was not significant in adsorption process, moreover, the fitting line failed to pass the origin, which indicated the intra-particle diffusion adsorption was not the control step of the adsorption process of 5-FU onto the NPs (Beheshti et al., 2016).

In optimum conditions, the loading capacity of different nanocarriers declined in order of CDs < Fe3O4 < FCCs < CS. The comparable and moderately higher loading capacity of MFNPs could be explained by the unique physical and chemical properties including high specific surface area, substantial surface active sites and flexible interface structure, which were provided by the surface-modification of CS/CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modeling of equilibrium data and thermodynamic parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
In this work, two sorption isotherms of Freundlich and Langmuir isotherm model (Freundlich, 1906; Langmuir, 1916) (Eqs. (5) and (6)) were applied to analyze the equilibrium data (Beheshti et al., 2016):
(5)
q e
=
k F
c e
1 n
(6)
q e
=
q m
K L
c e
1 +
K L
c e
where q e is the equilibrium adsorbing amount of 5-FU, c e is the equilibrium concentration of the 5-FU solution, k F and n is the Freundlich equilibrium constant, q m is the maximum adsorption capacity, and k L is the Langmuir equilibrium constant. Langmuir isothermal model is based on no interactions between adsorbent and adsorbate, a uniform monolayer adsorption, and the homogeneously distributed adsorption energy over the overall coverage surface. The relationship between the amounts of 5-FU absorbed per unit mass of Fe3O4 and FCCs versus the concentration of 5-FU remaining in solution at different temperatures were shown in Fig. 7 a and d, respectively. The correlation coefficients (R 2) and parameter values calculated from Langmuir isothermal model linear regression (Fig. 7b and e) were depicted in Table 2 . As shown, the high regression correlation coefficients (R 2 > 0.995) indicated that the Langmuir model was very appropriate for describing 5-FU equilibrium data by the NPs. Furthermore, the value of q m raised with increasing temperature. A higher value of k L also implied strong interaction of 5-FU and NPs in the research temperatures. The Freundlich isotherm model is an empirical equation based on absorption on a heterogeneous surface. The parameter values obtained from linear regression (Fig. 7c and f) were depicted in Table 2. As seen, the reciprocal values of the Freundlich constant (n−1) at all research temperatures were range from 0 to 1, demonstrating that the sorption of 5-FU using NPs was beneficial at studied conditions. Nevertheless, the Langmuir model was more appropriate to describe the 5-FU equilibrium data because the correlation coefficients of the Langmuir (R 2 > 0.99) was higher than Freundlich (R 2 > 0.63) models. Thus, the monolayer adsorption is the dominating adsorptive mode in these 5-FU sorption systems under the experimental conditions.

Thermodynamic parameters such as Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) entropy change of adsorption (ΔS) and the enthalpy change usually plays a vital role to determine the degrees of feasibility and spontaneity of sorption process. The ΔG of 5-FU adsorption process is reckoned by Eqs. (7) and (8):
(7)
Δ G = − RT ln
k
c
(8)
k c
=
lim
c
e l
→ 0
c
e s
c
e l
where R is the gas constant (8.314 J/mol K−1), T is an absolute temperature (K), k c is the adsorption equilibrium constant, c es and c el are the values of solid and liquid phase concentration in equilibrium (mg L−1), respectively. The relationship between the equilibrium constant (k c) and the temperature is given by the Van’t Hoff equation (Eq. (9)).
(9)
ln
k c
=
Δ
S
R
−
Δ
H
R T

The values of ΔS and ΔH of the studied system obtained from the slope and intercept of a Van’t Hoff plot of ln k c versus 1/T were showed in inset of Fig. 7a and d. Thermodynamic parameters for the adsorption process of 5-FU onto the NPs were presented in Table 2. As shown, all values of ΔG were negative at research conditions, proving the spontaneity and feasibility of 5-FU by NPs. In addition, the values of ΔG for 5-FU onto the NPs negatively decreased with increasing temperature, which indicated that the higher temperature was more feasible for the sorption. The positive values of ΔS proved the raised randomness in solid-solution interface during the sorption process. The positive values of ΔH confirmed the endothermic property of the 5-FU sorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release and coexisting substances influence
ce:section.ce:para: The 5-FU release from the MFNPs in PBS with different pH values simulated the in vivo environment was also investigated (Fig. 8 a). The release rate of 5-FU increased faster initial, and grow slower after. In the buffer solution of pH5.5, the 5-FU-Fe3O4 can release all loaded 5-FU after 6 h. When the pH value is 7.4, it needs about 18 h. The potential explanation is that the NH group in 5-FU molecule and H+ in the solution can form a NH2 + at a low pH, which facilitates the release of 5-FU (Ehi-Eromosele et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017). Moreover, the 5-FU release rate on Fe3O4 was fast than FCCs, which can be explained as the π–π stacking and hydrogen bonding between CS/CDs and 5-FU can be more stable. This comparison clearly confirms that the incorporation of CS/CDs could greatly delay the 5-FU release and avoid the burst release which often appears in the conventional natural polysaccharides-based NDS (Hubbell & Chilkoti, 2012). To research the release kinetics of the FCCs-5-FU, the resultant data were fitted by Higuchi model (Higuchi, 1961) and Korsmeyer-Peppas model (Korsmeyer, Gurny, Doelker, Buri, & Peppas, 1983), respectively. Higuchi model can be expressed by the following equation: M t/M ∞ = k Ht1/2, where M t/M ∞ represents the fraction of drug released at the time t, k H is the Higuchi constant. As depicted in Fig. 8b, a good linear relationship between M t/M ∞ and t 1/2 was observed for both two pH values (5.5, 7.4), confirming that the 5-FU release from the MFNPs was mainly controlled by Fickian diffusion. Korsmeyer-Peppas model (M t/M ∞ = k ptn) was also employed to study the release kinetics, where k p represents the Korsmeyer-Peppas rate constant and n is the exponent. Generally, when the value of n is <0.5 and >0.5, drug release process is controlled by Fickian diffusion and non-Fickian diffusion respectively. The data obtained from the 5-FU release were plotted as ln(M t/M ∞) versus lnt (Fig. 8c), which exhibited straight lines with slopes of n at both two pH values, the values of n were calculated to be 0.49 and 0.46 at pH 5.5 and 7.4, respectively, further suggesting that the release of 5-FU from the MFNPs fitted the Fickian diffusion controlled release mechanism at studied pH values.

Due to the complexity of substances in vivo, the abundant competitive species may largely deteriorate the release performance of 5-FU. Based on this, coexisting substances, such as chloridion, phosphate, carbonate and sodion at different concentration of 0, 0.1 and 0.5 M, equilibrium solution pH of 7.0, and release time of 30 min were examined, and the results were demonstrated in Fig. 8d. The results demonstrated that the FCCs was able to sustain release even when high concentrations of coexisting ions were present. The existence of sodion showed most significant promote effect on the release of 5-FU from NPs. However, the presence of carbonate, sulfate and phosphate exhibited relay effect on the release of 5-FU.

We report a facile high-gravity-assisted approach for the synthesis of MFNPs. The obtained multi-layer MFNPs showed superparamagnetic properties, good biocompatibility and superior fluorescent emission. The maximum loading capacity of 5-FU onto MFNPs can be reached 216.1 mg g−1. The monolayer adsorption is the main sorption mode in the studied system. The overall adsorption process is controlled by diffusion mass transfer in the liquid phase, as well as governed by chemisorption. The release of 5-FU from the MFNPs is controlled by Fickian diffusion. The advanced MFNPs has great potential for application in 5-FU delivery under fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Graduate Technological Innovation Project of Beijing Institute of Technology (grant numbers 2017CX10023). We also very appreciative of the technical support of Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Higee-Oriented Chemical Engineering.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2017.12.079.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
Details of IS-RPB, optimization of operating conditions on the performance of IS-RPB, and fitting parameters of Higuchi model and Korsmeyer-Peppas model.
